help.primaryWinnerCallout: Among all stage-1 rules whose match conditions fit the request, exactly one wins primary routing: the enabled rule with the highest numeric Priority (larger number wins). Other matching rules are not merged for the primary path. Rules whose strategy type is Fallback never win primary — they only add recovery targets after every primary upstream attempt fails.
help.strategyFallbackRecoveryOnly: Strategy type Fallback does not win primary routing at stage 1. Its targets are used only as recovery after all primary upstream attempts fail. For an ordered primary chain inside one rule, use Conditional with a nested Fallback tree under default.
help.strategyType: Controls how targets are chosen when the rule matches: single destination, ordered fallback, weighted load balancing, conditional tree, or A/B split. The JSON under Configuration must follow the same strategy shape the API validates.
help.priority: Among stage-1 rules that match the same request, the one with the highest Priority wins primary routing (larger number wins). Rules with strategy type Fallback never win primary — they only contribute recovery targets after all primary upstream attempts fail (still ordered by Priority among fallback rules). Use larger numbers for more specific or business-critical routes.
help.enabled: When off, the rule is skipped entirely. Matching traffic uses the next applicable rule or the model default route. Use disable instead of delete when you may re-enable later.
help.status: Enabled rules participate in routing when their match conditions fit the request. Disabled rules are stored but never evaluated.
help.configuration: The strategy payload the gateway loads when this rule matches: nested provider and model IDs, weights, and optional condition trees. This is the source of truth the runtime uses together with match conditions and priority.
help.configurationSingle: Every matching request is sent to exactly one provider and model pair shown here. IDs refer to records configured under Providers & Models.
help.configurationFallback: When this rule's strategy type is Fallback at stage 1, it does not win primary routing: its ordered targets are used only after every primary upstream attempt fails (then proxy walks the chain). Recovery targets are precomputed when the request is routed; the proxy only dials providers for them after primary targets are exhausted. For a primary route that still uses an ordered chain inside one rule, use Conditional with default set to a nested Fallback tree (see Pipeline stage help).
help.configurationLoadBalance: Requests are distributed randomly by relative weight across targets. Higher weight means a larger share of traffic. Sticky affinity (when stickyOn is set) is stored in shared Redis when the gateway has Redis configured so the same key sticks to the same target across replicas; without Redis, sticky state is per process only (fine for single instance / dev).
help.configurationConditional: Evaluates branches with "when" expressions against the live request context, then runs the matching "then" strategy or the mandatory "default". Use the structured editor for a default route, ordered branches (field path, operator, value, then target), and optional raw JSON for expressions the form cannot represent yet ($and / $or, nested strategies). Match conditions on the rule still decide whether this rule is considered at all.
help.configurationAbSplit: Weighted random choice among flat provider/model pairs — ideal for experiments comparing models or providers at a fixed traffic mix.
help.configurationSmart: Uses an AI model (the router) to analyze the user's request and automatically select the best model. Benefits: better model fit and cost/latency tradeoffs for mixed traffic. Costs/risks: extra router LLM call per model:auto request (latency and token spend); if the router fails or times out, the gateway uses Default Model; responses may still be cacheable under the chosen target model like any other route once routing completes.
help.configurationPolicy: Stage-0 rules only. Merges allow/deny lists for gateway model IDs and provider IDs before any stage-1 route rule runs. Allow-lists from multiple matching policy rules intersect; deny-lists union. Stage-1 targets and smart-router candidates must satisfy the effective narrowing.
help.pipelineStage: Choose whether this row is stage 0 (policy) or stage 1 (route). 0 — Policy: merged before any route; config must be allow/deny lists (internal gateway model and provider IDs). 1 — Route: evaluated after stage 0; among matches, the gateway picks the highest-Priority rule whose strategy type is not Fallback. Rules with strategy type Fallback supply the post-failure recovery chain only (ordered by Priority among those rules). Recovery targets are precomputed when the request is routed; the proxy only dials providers for them after primary targets are exhausted. To use an internal fallback chain as the primary route (e.g. Sonnet then Haiku), use strategy type Conditional with an empty conditions list and put the Fallback tree under default.
help.matchConditions: Narrows which requests may use this rule. All set fields are combined with AND. Models: resolved internal gateway model IDs. Providers: internal Provider.id UUIDs — the request's resolved model must belong to one of these providers. Organizations: VirtualKey.projectId values (UUIDs). Virtual keys: name patterns with optional asterisk wildcards. If you leave every list empty, matching falls back to gateway defaults — use with care.
help.matchModelsLabel: Gateway model IDs (not vendor API names). A request matches here when its routed model equals one of the selected IDs.

strategy.singleDesc: Routes every matching request to exactly one provider and model pair. The simplest strategy — no failover, no load distribution.
strategy.fallbackDesc: An ordered list of provider/model targets tried sequentially when primary routing fails (timeout, error, rate limit). Each target is attempted once before moving to the next. As a stage-1 strategy type, Fallback rules never win primary — they contribute recovery targets only.
strategy.loadbalanceDesc: Distributes requests randomly across targets by relative weight. Higher weight means more traffic share. Optionally supports sticky affinity via shared Redis.
strategy.conditionalDesc: Evaluates "when" expressions against the live request context and runs the matching "then" strategy or the mandatory "default". Supports nested strategies and complex branching logic.
strategy.abSplitDesc: Weighted random choice among flat provider/model pairs — ideal for experiments comparing models or providers at a fixed traffic mix.
strategy.smartDesc: Uses an AI router model to analyze the user's request and automatically select the best target model. Adds one extra LLM call per request; falls back to Default Model if the router fails.
strategy.policyDesc: Stage-0 only. Merges allow/deny lists for gateway model IDs and provider IDs before any stage-1 route rule runs. Allow-lists intersect; deny-lists union.

hooks.title: Hooks
hooks.subtitle: Built-in logic lives in gateway source; which hooks run, their order (priority), enablement, and labels are configured in the database.
hooks.deleteConfirm: Are you sure you want to delete hook "{{name}}"? This action cannot be undone.
hooks.typeTooltip: Built-in hooks run in-process; webhooks call your HTTPS endpoint. The type controls validation and failure handling for this row.
hooks.stageTooltip: Request hooks run before the upstream model call; response hooks run on the way back to the client.
hooks.priorityTooltip: Lower numbers run first among hooks at the same stage. Use this to order validation, limits, and transforms.
hooks.timeoutTooltip: Maximum wait for this hook (built-in work or webhook round-trip). Exceeding it counts as a hook failure per fail behavior.
hooks.failBehaviorTooltip: Fail-open continues the request if the hook errors or times out; fail-closed blocks traffic when the hook cannot complete.
hooks.enabledTooltip: Disabled hooks are skipped in the pipeline. Use for safe rollout or temporary suspension without deleting configuration.
hooks.dualPhaseValue: Can run in both request and response
hooks.webhookTargetHelp: Choose AIGuard for the built-in compliance endpoint, or keep custom for external webhooks.
hooks.noConfigData: No configuration data available for this hook.
hooks.pipelineUnknown: Unable to determine pipeline position. The hook may not be wired into the execution chain.
hooks.testDescription: JSON body for POST /hooks/:id/test. Use {"input":{ "prompt":"..." }} (prompt is mapped to body.messages) or full input with body.messages. Response-stage hooks also scan that request text against a synthetic assistant reply when sampleBody is omitted.
hooks.testRulePackTestNote: The AI gateway resolves enabled rule-pack installs from the database for this test, matching production behavior (not the raw JSON on the Configuration tab alone).
hooks.formTypeHelp: Built-in hooks run inside the gateway process. Webhooks call an external HTTPS endpoint you control.
hooks.formStageHelp: Request-stage hooks run before the upstream call; response-stage hooks run after the model replies.
hooks.formPriorityHelp: 1-100 (lower = runs first). Execution order among hooks at the same stage.
hooks.formTimeoutHelp: 100-30000ms (default 5000). Maximum time to wait for this hook.
hooks.formFailBehaviorHelp: Fail-open allows the request to continue if the hook errors. Fail-closed blocks traffic.
hooks.formEnabledTooltip: Disabled hooks are skipped entirely. Use while testing or rolling out changes without removing the configuration.
hooks.implementationFieldHelp: Registered gateway plugin (implementationId). The form below follows the JSON Schema published by this implementation when available.
hooks.noImplementationsMatch: No implementations match this type and stage. Adjust type or stage, or reload the registry.
hooks.endpointUrlHelp: Must be HTTPS and reachable from the gateway.
hooks.manualJsonTooltip: Schema-driven fields use the gateway's registered JSON Schema. Manual JSON is for advanced options or when no schema is published.
hooks.dualPhaseNote: You may add another row with the other stage for the same behavior.
hooks.categoryOverrideHint: Enable/disable, priority, stage, and category are stored in the database. Saving clears the gateway hook cache so changes apply on the next request.
hooks.noMatch: No hooks match the current filters.
hooks.createSubtitle: Configure a hook for the gateway pipeline.
hooks.pipelineDescription: Reflects current HookConfig rows in the database. After create/update/delete in this UI, the gateway clears its in-memory hook cache so the next proxied request uses the new order and enabled flags.
hooks.pipelineDualPhaseCapable: Can be configured for both request and response (separate rows)
hooks.pipelineEmptyPhase: No hooks configured for this phase.
hooks.pipelineFooterDecisions: Hook decisions (pipeline): approve, reject, modify, abstain — merged in order.
hooks.pipelineFooterOutcome: Response outcome (to client): pass, block, modify — mapped from the response pipeline after all hooks run.
hooks.pipelinePolicyNote: Hooks run in priority order (lower number = higher priority). Reject-hard short-circuits the pipeline.
hooks.pipelineOrderNote: Request hooks run before upstream. Response hooks run after upstream returns.
hooks.pipelineReorderHint: Use Up/Down to change priority for this phase; the gateway reloads hook order on save (cache invalidated).
hooks.applicableIngressHelp: Which ingress types this hook runs on. `ALL` matches every ingress; the specific codes restrict execution to a single service. Must not be empty.

rulePacks.installsSubtitle: Bind catalog rule packs to this hook. When at least one install is enabled, the gateway evaluates pack rules (merged in install order). Per-install overrides only affect that install row.
rulePacks.configPrecedenceBody: This hook has at least one enabled rule-pack install. The gateway merges rules from those installs (in install order) and evaluates them through the unified rule-pack engine. Inline pattern lists in the JSON below are ignored for matching.
rulePacks.configPrecedencePiiExtra: In-place PII redact (MODIFY) from legacy patternDefinitions is not available while rule packs are enabled; decisions follow pack severities (hard, soft, info) instead.
rulePacks.configPrecedenceSeeTab: Manage installs and per-install overrides on the Rule Packs tab.
rulePacks.configAllInstallsDisabledBody: Every rule-pack install on this hook is disabled. The gateway uses inline patterns from the JSON below (legacy path), same as when no packs are bound.
rulePacks.configNoInstallsBody: No rule packs are bound to this hook. Matching uses only the fields in the JSON below. Bind packs on the Rule Packs tab when you want catalog-managed rules instead.
rulePacks.overridesPerInstallScope: Overrides apply only to this install. Other installs on the same hook, or the same pack bound elsewhere, keep their own override rows.

credentials.statusTooltip: Reflects whether the gateway may select this credential for outbound calls to the provider.
credentials.rotationStateTooltip: Tracks automated or manual key rotation. Failures here usually mean the provider rejected the new secret or health checks did not pass.
credentials.storedSecretTooltip: The raw API key is encrypted at rest and is never shown again after save. Use rotation to replace it; revoke old keys at the vendor when done.

credentials.newApiKeyLabel: New API Key (leave empty to keep current)
credentials.newApiKeyHelpText: The dashboard never shows the existing key. Paste a new vendor secret to replace it; leave blank to keep the current encrypted value. Revoke the old key at the provider after a successful rotation.
credentials.rotationStateHelpText: Operational flag for key lifecycle workflows (pending validation, completed rotation, failures). Adjust only when you understand your runbook — incorrect values can block automated rotation jobs.
credentials.enabledTooltip: Disabled credentials are not used for new upstream calls. Existing in-flight requests may still complete; re-enable after fixing validation errors.
credentials.nameHelpTextCreate: Shown in lists and routing UIs. Choose a name your team will recognize when multiple keys exist for the same provider.
credentials.providerHelpTextCreate: The upstream vendor this secret belongs to. The gateway uses the provider's base URL and adapter when sending requests signed with this credential.
credentials.apiKeyHelpTextCreate: Paste the vendor secret once. It is encrypted at rest and is not shown again in the dashboard — rotate at the provider if you lose the value.
credentials.selectionWeightHelp: Higher weight means this credential receives proportionally more traffic when multiple credentials share a provider. Set to 0 to exclude from the pool without disabling.
credentials.poolStatusHelp: Controls whether this credential participates in pool selection. Active = eligible. Retiring = draining (no new traffic). Retired = awaiting deletion.
credentials.reliabilityColumnTooltip: Hover for the full breakdown: health 5 min, health 1 h, trend, dominant error, circuit state, opened-at, next-probe, live auth-fails. Health refreshes every 5 min, circuit state every 30 s.
credentials.rate5mIdle: idle · no traffic in last 5 min
credentials.samplesIdle: 0 in last 5 min · see 1h rate above
credentials.seeReliabilityTab: See the Reliability tab for the full breakdown
credentials.thresholdOverridesHelp: Leave a field blank to inherit the global value from Settings → Credential Reliability. Saving an empty form clears all overrides.
credentials.noOverrides: No per-credential overrides — using the global Settings values.
credentials.authFailThresholdHelp: Consecutive 401/403 responses before the circuit opens with reason auth_fail.
credentials.rateLimitCooldownSecondsHelp: How long a rate_limit OPEN circuit stays open before auto-transitioning to half_open.
credentials.healthyThresholdPctHelp: Success rate ≥ this percentage classifies the credential as healthy.
credentials.degradedThresholdPctHelp: Below this percentage the credential is classified as unavailable.
credentials.healthMinSamplesHelp: Below this sample count the credential shows as collecting (not yet classified).
credentials.healthWindowSecondsHelp: Rolling window for the current health classification. The trend uses 12× this window as baseline.
credentials.healthSustainedDegradedSecondsHelp: Time the credential must stay degraded before credential.health_degraded_sustained fires.

attestation.title: Agent Attestation
attestation.desc: When enabled, agents cryptographically attest that they already inspected each request, so the Compliance Proxy can transparently tunnel attested traffic (skipping its own MITM + hook pipeline). Verified attestations save ~30-50 ms per request and roughly halve CP CPU. Invalid or missing signatures always fall back to the existing full-MITM path — this is a performance optimization, not a security gate.
attestation.onHint: Agents will sign outbound requests; CP transparently tunnels on a valid signature.
attestation.offHint: Agents do not sign; every request flows through CP's full MITM + hook pipeline (current behavior).

settings.agentRuntimeDesc: Fleet-wide reporting cadence, updater channel, and log level. Leave a field empty to fall back to the agent's YAML default.

trafficUploadLevel.label: Traffic upload level
trafficUploadLevel.helpAll: Every flow is uploaded — including silent TCP passthroughs. Highest cost; useful for audit windows.
trafficUploadLevel.helpProcessed: Only flows the agent inspected (HTTP visible, adapter matched, or hook ran) reach Hub. Recommended for production.
trafficUploadLevel.helpBlocked: Only blocked / denied / errored flows reach Hub. Silent operation; compliance evidence preserved.

quicFallback.title: QUIC fallback bundles (macOS)
quicFallback.desc: macOS bundle IDs of apps whose UDP flows the agent will close to force HTTP/3 → HTTP/2 fallback. Browsers and Electron AI clients prefer h3 to QUIC-friendly endpoints (ChatGPT, Claude.ai, Cloudflare-fronted services); without this list the agent's TCP path never sees their requests. Add Chromium-based desktop apps you also want intercepted (Cursor, Claude Desktop, etc.). NEVER add system processes (com.apple.mDNSResponder, dhcpcd, etc.) — that breaks DNS and takes the host network down.
quicFallback.empty: No bundles configured — agent will not close any UDP flows.
quicFallback.howToFind: Find a Mac app's bundle ID with: defaults read /Applications/SomeApp.app/Contents/Info.plist CFBundleIdentifier

bypassBundles.title: Exempted bundles (macOS)
bypassBundles.desc: macOS bundle IDs of apps the agent will pass through WITHOUT inspection. Use only for a trusted tool whose certificate-pinned TLS genuinely breaks under inspection (e.g. a developer CLI). Matching is by source app, never by destination — so the same host stays inspected from every other app. Leave empty unless you have a specific, reviewed reason.
bypassBundles.warning: ⚠ Apps listed here are NOT inspected for compliance. Their AI traffic produces no audit records and is invisible to policy. Every entry is a deliberate, reviewed compliance carve-out — keep this list as short as possible, and remove entries once they are no longer needed.
bypassBundles.empty: No apps exempted — everything is inspected (recommended).
bypassBundles.howToFind: Find a Mac app's bundle ID with: defaults read /Applications/SomeApp.app/Contents/Info.plist CFBundleIdentifier

ruleDesc.openaiFieldOrder: Canonicalise top-level JSON field order for OpenAI-wire bodies so cache keys are stable across SDKs (Python / Go / TypeScript emit different orderings).

costs.title: Costs breakdown
costs.internalOpsNoteCounted: Semantic cache embedding + AI-Guard are real-money internal-ops costs. They are currently COUNTED against this VK's quota (operator pays the upstream bills regardless of who triggered them). To exclude them, set excludeInternalOpsFromBilledCost: true in nexus-hub.yaml.
costs.internalOpsNoteExcluded: Semantic cache embedding + AI-Guard are gateway internal-ops costs. They are currently EXCLUDED from this VK's quota; the operator absorbs them separately. To include them, set excludeInternalOpsFromBilledCost: false in nexus-hub.yaml.

tier.hint: Which decoder claimed this row: Tier 1 = exact protocol decoder (matched by key, host, or content sniff); Tier 2 = pattern probe for consumer web surfaces; Structural = typed projection of the raw HTTP body, no protocol decode; no badge = legacy raw verbatim row. "host-matched" replaces the confidence number when the adapter was chosen by host: its coverage is honest but not a trust score comparable to a sniffed decode.

cacheRoi.title: Cache ROI
cacheRoi.subtitle: Prompt cache cost savings and normaliser impact across all providers.
cacheRoi.combinedSavingsSub: gateway response cache + provider prompt cache
cacheRoi.gatewaySavingsSub: full response cache — no provider call
cacheRoi.noData: No cache data for this period.
cacheRoi.savingsRateNullTooltip: No write cost in this period — the cache was created in a prior window. Read savings still apply.
cacheRoi.savingsRateFormula: Formula: net savings ÷ gross cost. Net savings = Gateway Cache savings + Provider Prompt Cache net savings (read discount minus write overhead). Gross cost = actual spend + net savings = what you would have paid with no caching at all. Example: would have spent $100, only spent $10 → 90% savings rate.
cacheRoi.tooltipGatewayCache: Gateway response cache stores normalized request → response. Identical subsequent requests are served without calling the upstream — full upstream cost avoided.
cacheRoi.tooltipProviderLayer: Provider prompt cache (e.g. Anthropic cache_control). The request still reaches the provider, but previously-seen prompt tokens are billed at a reduced rate. Savings = (standard rate − cache rate) × cached tokens.
cacheRoi.tooltipCombinedSavings: Total cost avoided: Gateway Cache savings (cost = $0 per hit) plus Provider Prompt Cache net savings (reduced token price on cached input).
cacheRoi.sectionGatewayDesc: Full responses served from platform cache — no provider API call
cacheRoi.sectionProviderDesc: Token-level KV cache at provider — request reaches provider at reduced billing rate
cacheRoi.rollupNotReady: Metrics are computed from raw event data — rollup tables are empty for this period. Run rollup jobs to build aggregated data (faster queries, longer retention).
cacheRoi.rollupTriggered: Jobs triggered — data ready in ~2 min

analytics.latencyAreaSubtitle: Average ms per phase per bucket, stacked. Empty until the Hub rollup correction job has run after backfill.

analytics.latencyHistoricalTitle: Some phases show no data in this window
analytics.latencyHistoricalBody: Upstream TTFB and response_hooks phases are recorded from 2026-05-14 onward. Rows older than that report total latency only — the backfill cannot reconstruct first-byte timing or per-hook durations. Narrow the time window to last 24 h to see fully populated phase data.
analytics.latencyStackedSubtitle: Each bar is one provider, segmented by where the time was spent at P95.
analytics.latencyLeaderboardSubtitle: Sorted by P95 upstream total — the worst provider experiences are at the top.

settingsPayloadCapture.title: Payload Capture
settingsPayloadCapture.subtitle: Control whether request and response bodies are stored with each traffic event. Captured bodies appear in audit detail views and the traffic_event_payload table.
settingsPayloadCapture.streamingNote: Response body capture covers both streaming (SSE) and non-streaming responses.
settingsPayloadCapture.maxBytesHelp: Bodies up to this size are stored inline in the database. Larger bodies are stored as files in the configured spill backend. Does NOT affect bytes forwarded upstream. Values above 10 MB are clamped server-side. Default: 262144 (256 KiB).
settingsPayloadCapture.maxRequestBytesHelp: Bounds the bytes the data plane reads from the inbound request. Oversized requests are rejected with HTTP 413 before reaching any provider. Values above 100 MB are clamped server-side. Default: 10485760 (10 MiB).
settingsPayloadCapture.maxResponseBytesHelp: Bounds the bytes the data plane reads from a non-streaming upstream response. Overflow surfaces as HTTP 502 (upstream_error). Streaming responses are unaffected. Values above 100 MB are clamped server-side. Default: 10485760 (10 MiB).
settingsPayloadCapture.confirmEnableDesc: Turning this on means request or response bodies — which may contain user prompts, PII, or other sensitive content — will be written to the traffic_event_payload table. Confirm that your retention and access policies are set before enabling.

cliSetup.downloadHint: Pick the build for your platform. Each is a single static executable — no installer required.
cliSetup.installMacOSStep1:
# Apple Silicon shown; for Intel swap arm64 → amd64
curl -fL {{downloadBase}}/downloads/nexus-cli-darwin-arm64-latest -o nexus
# Verify the download against the published checksum before running it
echo "$(curl -fsSL {{downloadBase}}/downloads/nexus-cli-darwin-arm64-latest.sha256)  nexus" | shasum -a 256 -c -
cliSetup.installMacOSStep2:
# The binary is not notarized — clear the Gatekeeper quarantine flag, then make it executable
xattr -d com.apple.quarantine ./nexus 2>/dev/null; chmod +x ./nexus
cliSetup.installMacOSStep3:
# Move it onto your PATH
sudo mv ./nexus /usr/local/bin/nexus
cliSetup.installLinuxStep1:
curl -fL {{downloadBase}}/downloads/nexus-cli-linux-amd64-latest -o nexus
# Verify the download against the published checksum before running it
echo "$(curl -fsSL {{downloadBase}}/downloads/nexus-cli-linux-amd64-latest.sha256)  nexus" | sha256sum -c -
chmod +x ./nexus
cliSetup.installWindowsStep1:
# PowerShell — download to the current folder
Invoke-WebRequest {{downloadBase}}/downloads/nexus-cli-windows-amd64-latest.exe -OutFile nexus.exe
# Verify the download against the published checksum before running it
$expected = (Invoke-WebRequest {{downloadBase}}/downloads/nexus-cli-windows-amd64-latest.exe.sha256 -UseBasicParsing).Content.Trim()
if ((Get-FileHash nexus.exe -Algorithm SHA256).Hash -ine $expected) { throw 'nexus.exe checksum mismatch — do not run it' }
cliSetup.installWindowsStep2: # Move nexus.exe into a folder on your PATH (e.g. %LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs), then reopen your terminal

agentSetup.downloadHintMacOS: Distribute the signed + notarized .pkg via your software-distribution channel (Jamf, Kandji, an internal share, GitHub Releases, etc.). End users install with a double-click.
agentSetup.downloadHintWindows: WiX v4 MSI installer (signed once Authenticode cert is provisioned). Double-click runs a standard Windows wizard: registers Nexus Agent as a Windows Service, installs WinDivert kernel driver, sets system-wide env vars NEXUS_DEVICE_CA_PEM + NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS + REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE + SSL_CERT_FILE so Node.js / Python / Go / curl trust the agent's intercepted TLS. Suitable for GPO / SCCM / Intune distribution.
agentSetup.downloadHintLinux: Cross-compiled raw Go binary (no .deb/.rpm packaging yet). Operators install it manually under a systemd unit — see Step 2. Suitable for headless servers and dev workstations alike.
agentSetup.installMacOSStep1: Double-click NexusAgent-<VERSION>.pkg. The installer is signed by YOUR ORG NAME and notarized by Apple — Gatekeeper allows it without warnings.
agentSetup.installMacOSStep2: Provide an admin password when prompted (the package needs to register a LaunchDaemon as root).
agentSetup.installMacOSStep3: macOS shows a 'System Extension Blocked' notification on first launch. Open System Settings → General → Login Items & Extensions → Network Extensions and approve com.nexus-gateway.agent.extension from YOUR ORG NAME. (For MDM-managed fleets, deploy the .mobileconfig template that ships with the agent to pre-approve.)
agentSetup.installMacOSStep4: On first launch the postinstall step generates a device CA and installs it into the System Keychain as a trusted root anchor — this is what makes the agent's intercepted TLS connections trusted by every local HTTPS client.
agentSetup.installWindowsStep1: Double-click NexusAgent-<VERSION>.msi. Windows shows the standard installer wizard (Welcome → License → Install Location → Confirm → Install → Finish). Click through; UAC will prompt for elevation once because the MSI registers a system service and a kernel driver.
agentSetup.installWindowsStep2: The wizard installs: nexus-agent.exe (service) + nexus-agent-tray.exe (login auto-start) + nexus-dashboard.exe (start-menu shortcut) under C:\Program Files\Nexus Agent\; WinDivert64.sys + WinDivert.dll for kernel packet capture; %ProgramData%\NexusAgent\ for device CA + config + logs.
agentSetup.installWindowsStep3: On first service start the daemon mints the device CA at %ProgramData%\NexusAgent\device-ca.pem and imports it into the Windows LocalMachine Root store via certutil. The MSI also sets system-wide env vars NEXUS_DEVICE_CA_PEM / NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS / REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE / SSL_CERT_FILE so Node.js (Claude CLI, Cursor, Electron apps), Python (requests, boto3, pip), Go, and curl all trust the agent's intercepted TLS without per-app config. Log out and log back in (or run `refreshenv` in any cmd / pwsh session) for already-running processes to pick the env vars up.
agentSetup.installWindowsStep4: Verify: in an elevated PowerShell run `sc.exe query NexusAgent` (expect STATE: 4 RUNNING) and `Get-Item Env:NEXUS_DEVICE_CA_PEM` (expect C:\ProgramData\NexusAgent\device-ca.pem). The tray icon turns green within a few seconds. If WinDivert refused to load (rare, mostly SecureBoot / corporate-EDR conflicts), the tray turns yellow and the agent degrades to system-proxy fallback — see the Dashboard's Diagnostics page for the Win32 error code.
agentSetup.installLinuxStep1Rpm: # Same binary works on every glibc Linux (Debian / Ubuntu / RHEL / Fedora / SUSE / Amazon Linux). No distro-specific packaging today.
agentSetup.installLinuxStep2: Create a systemd unit at /etc/systemd/system/nexus-agent.service: [Unit] After=network.target / [Service] ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/nexus-agent run --config /etc/nexus-agent/agent.yaml User=root Restart=always / [Install] WantedBy=multi-user.target. Then sudo systemctl daemon-reload && sudo systemctl enable --now nexus-agent.
agentSetup.installLinuxStep3: Generate the device CA + install it as a system trust anchor: sudo nexus-agent install-ca. This writes /var/lib/nexus-agent/device-ca.{pem,key}, copies the cert to /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/nexus-agent.crt, then runs update-ca-certificates (Debian/Ubuntu) or update-ca-trust (RHEL/Fedora) so curl/wget/Python requests trust the agent's leaf certs.
agentSetup.installLinuxStep4: Traffic interception uses iptables REDIRECT rules the agent installs and self-heals at runtime. Nothing else for the operator to configure.
agentSetup.enrollSSOMacOS: Open the Nexus Agent menu bar item → Open Setup. The Dashboard opens to its Onboarding page; click Sign in and complete SSO in the browser. The agent auto-enrolls and begins protecting traffic.
agentSetup.enrollSSOWindows: Open the Nexus Agent tray → Open Setup. Same Dashboard SSO flow as macOS.
agentSetup.enrollSSOLinux: Run `sudo nexus-agent enroll-sso` (or on a desktop install, open the Dashboard from the tray). The Hub URL is already in /etc/nexus-agent/agent.yaml so no flag is required.
agentSetup.verifyHint: The newly-enrolled device appears in Infrastructure → Nodes within a few seconds. The agent's tray icon turns green; the Dashboard's main pages (Overview / Activity / Identity / Diagnostics / Settings) become reachable.
agentSetup.verifyError: Couldn't load your enrolled devices. The Agent Setup page will keep retrying.
agentSetup.verifyEmptyHint: Once you finish steps 1–3 above and the agent calls home, this panel will list your machine automatically (refreshes every 5 seconds).

upgrade.q: How do I upgrade the agent?
upgrade.a: macOS: re-install the signed .pkg over the existing install — LaunchDaemon and System Extension reload automatically. Windows: install the newer .msi; WiX MajorUpgrade replaces the old version in place and re-registers the service. Linux: re-run install.sh or `apt/yum upgrade`. The device CA is preserved across upgrades, so trusted clients stay trusted; admin-pushed configuration is also preserved.

uninstall.q: How do I fully uninstall and wipe all data?
uninstall.a: Run the platform uninstaller (`sudo bash uninstall.sh` on macOS/Linux; Apps & Features → Nexus Agent → Uninstall on Windows), then manually wipe leftover data: `rm -rf ~/.nexus` on macOS/Linux; `Remove-Item -Recurse 'C:\ProgramData\NexusAgent'` on Windows. To revoke trust of the device CA: `security delete-certificate -c nexus-agent-device-ca /Library/Keychains/System.keychain` (macOS), `certutil -delstore Root nexus-agent-device-ca` (Windows), or delete `/usr/local/share/ca-certificates/nexus-agent.crt` then `update-ca-certificates` (Linux).

serviceWontStart.q: The service is installed but the daemon won't start
serviceWontStart.a: Check service state and tail the log. macOS: `sudo launchctl print system/com.nexus-gateway.agent` + `tail /Library/Logs/com.nexus-gateway.agent/agent.log`. Windows: `sc.exe queryex NexusAgent` + tail `%ProgramData%\NexusAgent\Logs\agent.log`. Linux: `systemctl status nexus-agent` + `journalctl -u nexus-agent -n 100`. Most common causes: (1) `hubURL` in agent.yaml is unreachable (firewall/DNS); (2) port 19080 or the IPC socket is already bound by a prior crashed daemon; (3) the device-CA path isn't writable; (4) enrollment token expired — re-enroll via SSO.

nodeSelfSigned.q: Claude CLI / Node.js script reports SELF_SIGNED_CERT_IN_CHAIN
nodeSelfSigned.a: Root cause is the same on all three platforms: the installer sets NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS to point at the device CA, but Node only reads environment variables when its process starts. Any Node process (terminal, IDE, Electron app, Claude CLI) that was already running before the agent install must be restarted. Verify in a fresh shell: `echo $NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` (macOS / Linux) or `$env:NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` (PowerShell). The value should be the path to device-ca.pem and the file must exist.

pythonCertVerify.a: Same root cause as the Node SSL issue: REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE was set system-wide but the Python interpreter / venv started before the agent. Restart the interpreter. If your code uses requests with certifi explicitly (no env override), pass `verify=os.environ['NEXUS_DEVICE_CA_PEM']` to each call, OR install `pip-system-certs` once (`pip install pip-system-certs`) so pip and requests both honor the OS Root store directly.

browserCertWarn.q: My browser shows a certificate warning on Google / GitHub / regular sites
browserCertWarn.a: This should NOT happen — the agent only bumps TLS for hosts you've explicitly added to Interception Domains. If a normal site is being bumped, an admin probably added an overly broad wildcard (such as `*` or `*.com`). Open Infrastructure → Interception Domains, find and remove the offending rule. The agent syncs within ~30 seconds; affected browser tabs need a reload to clear cached cert decisions.

browserAINotCaptured.q: Browser traffic to chatgpt.com / claude.ai / gemini.google.com isn't captured
browserAINotCaptured.a: Two likely causes: (1) the destination isn't on Interception Domains — go to Infrastructure → Interception Domains and add the host with action `inspect` or `process`. (2) Chrome and Firefox default to HTTP/3 over QUIC (UDP), which the agent kills so the browser falls back to TCP + HTTP/2; you'll see a small (~100ms) one-time delay then traffic flows normally. If a domain is on the list but still not captured, check Activity for BUMP_FAILED_PASSTHROUGH rows (next question).

cursorBumpFailed.q: Activity shows BUMP_FAILED_PASSTHROUGH for Cursor or Anthropic SDK calls
cursorBumpFailed.a: Some upstreams (Cursor's api2.cursor.sh, parts of Cloudflare with strict bot detection) reject the agent's vanilla TLS probe. The agent fails open — relays the connection opaquely so the user's app keeps working — and stamps BUMP_FAILED_PASSTHROUGH on the flow for visibility. Headers and body are not captured for those rows, but enrollment counts and host-level usage still aggregate. Long-term fix is to probe with a uTLS Chrome fingerprint — file a feature request mentioning the specific host.

sshGitTransparent.q: Does the agent break SSH, git push, MySQL, or other non-HTTPS protocols?
sshGitTransparent.a: No. The bridge automatically passes through any flow on a non-TLS port (anything other than 443 / 8443 and admin-configured HTTPS ports). SSH (22), git+ssh, SMTP (25/465/587), MySQL (3306), Postgres (5432), Redis (6379), MongoDB (27017), and similar all flow transparently without inspection. The agent only attempts TLS bumping when both the destination host is on Interception Domains AND the port is configured as TLS.

hubConnLost.q: Agent shows out of sync, or last_seen_at is stale on the Nodes page
hubConnLost.a: Open Dashboard → Diagnostics for the device, or Infrastructure → Nodes → click the device → see last error. Most common cause: corporate firewall is blocking the outbound WebSocket to `hub.<your-domain>:443`. The agent uses wss:// primarily and falls back to long-polling HTTPS on the same host:port, both on TCP 443 outbound. Have IT allow-list the Hub hostname. The agent retries every 30 seconds with exponential backoff; recovery is automatic once the network path is restored.

performance.q: What's the performance overhead of having the agent in the network path?
performance.a: Bumped flows (inspect or process action): ~5–15 ms added per connection for the TLS double-handshake; throughput after first byte is near wire speed. Passthrough flows: <1 ms (opaque TCP relay; no decryption). Memory: ~80 MB baseline + ~50 KB per active flow. CPU: <2% on a typical AI-dev workstation handling ~50 LLM requests/minute. On macOS the Network Extension adds negligible per-packet overhead in passthrough mode.

auditQueueHigh.q: The Dashboard's AUDIT QUEUE counter is high — should I worry?
auditQueueHigh.a: Normal during the first minute or two after a daemon restart (catching up on buffered SQLite rows). The agent auto-throttles at 500 unsent events — new flows go to passthrough until the queue drains, preventing memory blowup. If the queue stays above 1000 for more than 10 minutes, the Hub upload pipe is stuck — see the Hub unreachable question. The current build also wipes audit.db on fresh install, so a brand-new install should report 0.

logsWhere.q: Where are the agent logs and what should I grep for?
logsWhere.a: macOS: `/Library/Logs/com.nexus-gateway.agent/agent.log`. Windows: `%ProgramData%\NexusAgent\Logs\agent.log`. Linux: `journalctl -u nexus-agent` (or `/var/log/nexus-agent/` if you configured a file logger). Useful greps: `connection decision` for every bridge accept/reject; `hook decision` for each hook outcome (APPROVE / REJECT_HARD / BLOCK_SOFT); `BumpStatus` for TLS bump success or failure; `audit emit` for each row written to local SQLite.

systemExtensionBlocked.q: Menu bar shows yellow “Attention Needed — Network filter not connected” after install
systemExtensionBlocked.a: macOS 26 (Tahoe) splits System Extension activation from Network Extension filter activation. After approving the system extension prompt on first install, you MUST also go to System Settings → General → Login Items & Extensions → Network Extensions and toggle “Nexus Agent (YOUR ORG NAME)” ON. Without this second toggle the proxy provider process never starts. First install always trips this; macOS remembers the choice across upgrades.

windivertFailed.q: WinDivert refused to load — tray turned yellow
windivertFailed.a: Most common causes: (1) SecureBoot is enforcing strict cross-cert policy and rejects WinDivert64.sys's signature chain — disable SecureBoot or have IT push the cross-cert via GPO. (2) A corporate EDR (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender for Endpoint) is blocking unknown kernel-mode drivers — request a per-vendor allow rule for WinDivert v2.2.2 published by basil00. (3) Another packet-capture tool (Wireshark/npcap, Sysmon) already holds the same kernel slot. While WinDivert is unavailable the agent runs in system-proxy fallback mode (HTTP CONNECT) — most traffic still flows but UDP / QUIC capture is unavailable.

msiHang.q: MSI install or uninstall hangs at “Starting services” / “Stopping services”
msiHang.a: Two known causes: (1) the agent service is still running and WinDivert holds a divert handle — open Services.msc, stop NexusAgent first, then re-run the uninstaller. (2) WinDivert SCM service got stuck in STOP_PENDING — `sc.exe queryex WinDivert` shows PID 0 if so; a reboot is the clean recovery. Never force-delete the kernel service with `sc delete` while it is STOP_PENDING — risks DRIVER_POWER_STATE_FAILURE BSOD on next boot.

whyRoot.q: Why does the agent need root on Linux? Does it stay running as root?
whyRoot.a: Three steps require root at install time: (1) install the device CA into `/usr/local/share/ca-certificates/` and run `update-ca-certificates`; (2) install iptables NAT REDIRECT rules to capture outbound traffic; (3) bind to localhost ports. The daemon itself drops privileges to the unprivileged `nexus-agent` system user after these one-time setup tasks finish. Confirm with `ps -fC nexus-agent` — the running process should show user `nexus-agent`, not root.

firewallReload.q: iptables rules disappeared after firewalld / ufw reload — is the agent broken?
firewallReload.a: No — the agent self-heals. The runtime detects missing iptables NAT rules every 30 seconds and re-installs them. If you reload firewalld every minute you'll see a brief gap in interception; otherwise it's invisible. To make the rules persist across reload, save with `iptables-save > /etc/iptables/nexus-agent.rules` after first agent start, or add nexus-agent's nat table additively into firewalld's permanent rules.

identityProvider.title: Identity Provider
identityProvider.subtitle: External identity providers (Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace, ...) the platform trusts for single sign-on and SCIM user/group provisioning. Nexus is the Service Provider; configure each IdP at the IdP vendor first, then register it here.
identityProvider.localIdpNote: Built-in local user store. Does not receive SCIM pushes and has no external group mappings.
identityProvider.confirmDeleteIdpBody: Users linked to this IdP will lose access on their next request. SCIM tokens scoped to this IdP will be revoked. This action cannot be undone.

emptyState.title: No external identity providers yet
emptyState.body: Add your first IdP to let your team sign in with their company account. Until then, the built-in local password fallback remains active for admin sign-in.

howItWorks.title: How it works
howItWorks.intro: Nexus is the Service Provider (SP). Your team's identity provider (Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace, ...) handles authentication; Nexus trusts the IdP's assertions to log users in and to receive user/group provisioning pushes.
howItWorks.step1Body: OIDC or SAML — Nexus redirects users to the IdP, which authenticates them and posts an assertion back. New users can be auto-provisioned (JIT) on first sign-in.
howItWorks.step2Body: Your IdP pushes user + group changes to Nexus in real time using the SCIM token shown below. Joiners and leavers are reflected in seconds.
howItWorks.step3Title: Map IdP groups to IAM permissions
howItWorks.step3Body: Each external group (e.g. okta-engineering) maps to a Nexus IAM group that carries policy. SCIM-pushed members land in the mapped IAM group automatically.
howItWorks.localFallbackNote: If no external IdP is enabled, Nexus's built-in local password store remains available so admins can still sign in to recover the environment.

wizard.title: Add Identity Provider
wizard.intro: Connect an external IdP (Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace, ...) so your team can sign in with their company account. Nexus is the Service Provider; the IdP is configured at the IdP vendor first.
wizard.issuerHelp: Base URL of the IdP. Nexus uses its /.well-known/openid-configuration for discovery.
wizard.clientSecretPlaceholder: Provided by the IdP when you registered the app
wizard.redirectUriHelp: Register this exact URL with the IdP. Defaults to this Nexus host.
wizard.scopesHelp: Space-separated. openid is required; profile/email/groups recommended.
wizard.entityIdHelp: The IdP entity (Issuer) — shown on the IdP app config page.
wizard.ssoUrlHelp: IdP-side endpoint where Nexus POSTs AuthnRequests.
wizard.certPemHelp: X.509 certificate the IdP uses to sign assertions. Paste the PEM block.
wizard.metadataImportHelp: Paste the IdP's SAML metadata XML to auto-fill Entity ID, SSO URL, signing certificate, and detected email/groups attributes. You can still edit any field afterwards.
wizard.metadataImported: Metadata imported — review the fields below.
wizard.emailAttributeHelp: Assertion attribute carrying the user's email. Leave blank to use the default ("email") plus runtime detection of common claim names.
wizard.groupsAttributeHelp: Assertion attribute carrying group memberships (mapped via group mappings). Leave blank to use the default ("groups") plus runtime detection.
wizard.ssoParamsHelp: Extra key/value pairs appended to the IdP SSO URL on sign-in — e.g. Auth0 Organizations requires `organization`.
wizard.editSecretHelp: Leave as "********" to keep the saved value; type a new secret to replace it.
wizard.defaultRoleHelp: IAM group every auto-provisioned user joins as a baseline, on top of any external-group mappings.
wizard.defaultCpAccess: Grant Control Plane access by default
wizard.defaultCpAccessHelp: When on, users auto-provisioned through this IdP can sign in to the Control Plane. Leave off for IdPs that federate agent end-users.
wizard.authorizeParamsHelp: Extra key/value pairs appended to the IdP sign-in request — e.g. Auth0 organization, or prompt / connection / audience.

identityProvider.scimEndpointHelp: Configure your identity provider to push users and groups to this endpoint using the token below.
identityProvider.tokenCreatedWarning: Copy this token now. It will not be shown again.
identityProvider.confirmRevoke: Revoke this token? Any identity provider using it will lose SCIM access immediately.
identityProvider.noTokens: No active tokens. Generate one to enable SCIM provisioning.
identityProvider.groupMappingsHelp: Map identity provider groups to IAM groups to control permissions for synced users.
identityProvider.mappingFieldsRequired: External Group ID and IAM Group are required
identityProvider.confirmDeleteMapping: Delete this group mapping? Users in the external group will no longer inherit the mapped IAM group's permissions.

passthrough.title: Emergency Passthrough
passthrough.subtitle: 3-tier kill-switch that bypasses compliance hooks, response cache, and traffic normalization. Use only during active incidents — every enabled override auto-disables at its expiresAt.

banner.inactiveBody: All traffic is going through the normal compliance + cache + normalize pipeline.
banner.activeBody: Traffic matching the rows below is currently skipping one or more pipeline stages. Disable or wait for auto-expiry.

global.title: Global override
global.subtitle: Applies to every provider unless an adapter-level or provider-level override matches first.

adapter.title: Adapter overrides
adapter.subtitle: Per-adapter overrides take precedence over the global tier and are themselves overridden by provider-level entries.
adapter.adapterTypeHint: The provider's adapterType this override applies to (e.g. anthropic, openai).
adapter.deleteConfirm: Delete the passthrough override for adapter {{adapter}}?

provider.title: Provider overrides
provider.subtitle: Provider-level overrides are the most specific tier and take precedence over adapter + global.
provider.providerHint: The specific Provider this override applies to.
provider.deleteConfirm: Delete the passthrough override for provider {{provider}}?

fields.enabledHint: Master switch for this tier row. Off means this row is inactive even if it has bypass flags set.
fields.bypassHooksHint: Skip the compliance pipeline (PII detection, AI Guard, rule packs). Traffic flows through with no compliance checks.
fields.bypassCacheHint: Skip response-cache lookup AND write. Every request hits the upstream provider.
fields.bypassNormalizeHint: Skip TransformSpan emission to traffic_event_normalized. Forensic visibility drops. Requires bypassCache=true (cache keys are derived from the normalized payload).
fields.expiresAtHint: Maximum {{hours}}h from now. The Hub reconcile job automatically flips enabled=false at this time, so leaving incident-response cleanup to humans is not required.
fields.reasonPlaceholder: Describe why emergency passthrough is needed
fields.reasonHint: {{count}} of {{min}} chars. Required for audit; appears on every traffic_event row that was bypassed by this tier.

validation.passthrough_no_bypass_selected: At least one bypass flag must be set when the override is enabled.
validation.passthrough_normalize_requires_cache_byp: Bypass normalize requires bypass cache (the cache key is derived from the normalized payload).
validation.passthrough_invalid_expiry: Expires at is required and must be between now and now + 8 hours.
validation.passthrough_invalid_reason: Reason must be at least 20 characters long.

confirm.title: Confirm emergency passthrough
confirm.body: You are about to enable a kill-switch that bypasses parts of the gateway pipeline. Please verify the details below.

passthrough.noPermissionToEnable: You do not have permission to change passthrough state. Read-only access.

toasts.saveError: Failed to save passthrough. Check Hub health if you saw a propagation error.

cache.title: Cache
cache.subtitle: Fleet-wide configuration for gateway and provider cache, including freshness rules.
cache.saved: Configuration saved. Index rebuild in progress.

recentFeedback.title: Recent feedback
recentFeedback.subtitle: Last 100 "Mark as bad cache hit" submissions. Buffer is in-process and resets on Control Plane restart; poison entries are durable in Redis.

sectionGateway.title: Gateway Cache
sectionGateway.subtitle: Nexus-managed response cache — exact and semantic lookups, plus freshness rules that bypass the cache for time-sensitive prompts.

sectionProvider.title: Provider Prompt Cache
sectionProvider.subtitle: Upstream provider cache (Anthropic, OpenAI, ...). Nexus marks requests with cache_control / ephemeral hints; the provider decides whether to cache and serve.

providerPrompt.title: Provider prompt cache settings
providerPrompt.subtitle: Normaliser, adapter knobs, and rules that shape outbound requests for maximum upstream cache hit rate.

embedding.cardSubtitle: Required for semantic cache. Changing the model triggers a full Valkey index rebuild.
embedding.pickerHelpText: Only providers with at least one embedding-type model are listed. Add new entries on the Providers page.
embedding.reindexWarning: Changing the embedding model triggers a full Valkey index rebuild. Existing cached entries will be invalidated until the next request re-populates them.
embedding.probeLatencyTooltip: Embedding latency budget is 100ms — calls exceeding this hard-timeout fall through to the broker (semantic cache disabled per-request). Provider/network values near the budget signal that the semantic hits rate will be lower than capacity.
embedding.statusIndexNameTooltip: Auto-versioned (:v1 → :v2 → ...) on every embedding-model swap to enable blue/green index switching.
embedding.rebuildDescription: You're about to change the embedding model from {{oldModel}} to {{newModel}}. This drops the current semantic-cache index and rebuilds it with the new model's dimension ({{oldDim}}D → {{newDim}}D). Cached responses become unmatchable; the gateway will start writing fresh entries on the next eligible request. Continue?

semantic.cardSubtitle: Approximate-match response lookup based on embedding similarity. Requires an embedding model above.
semantic.killSwitchDisabledTooltip: Configure a provider and model first.
semantic.thresholdHelp: Cosine similarity gate for semantic hits. Range 0–1. Tune up for precision-sensitive workloads (RAG ≈ 0.98); tune down for paraphrase-tolerant creative writing (≈ 0.92). Default 0.96.
semantic.varyByHelp: Controls cross-tenant cache reuse. "vk" (default) — isolate by virtual key (single-tenant). "user"/"org" — typical multi-tenant. "none" — allow cross-tenant hits (use with care).
semantic.embedStrategyHelp: How the embedding input is shaped from the canonical request. Default "system_plus_last_user" works for most workloads; pick "last_user" for single-turn Q&A, "recent_turns" for multi-turn agent dialogs.
semantic.allowCrossModelHelp: When ON, an semantic hits cached against one upstream model can serve a request routed to a different model. Default OFF (model-specific). Turn ON for smart-routing or multi-model interchangeable workloads.

prewarm.successToast: Pre-warm complete. Corpus entries are now live in semantic cache.
prewarm.errorToast: Pre-warm request failed. Check the console for details.

freshness.title: Freshness rules
freshness.subtitle: Patterns that bypass gateway cache. When the user's prompt matches, the request is fetched fresh from upstream to avoid serving stale answers (stock prices, weather, news, ...).
freshness.deleteDescription: Delete rule "{{id}}"? This action cannot be undone.
freshness.fieldLanguagesHint: Comma-separated BCP-47 tags (e.g. en,zh). Empty = all languages.
freshness.errorKeywordsRequired: At least one keyword is required.
freshness.testSubtitle: Paste a sample prompt to see which rule (if any) would fire and skip the cache.
freshness.testPlaceholder: e.g. What is the stock price of AAPL today?
freshness.testResultMatch: Matched rule {{ruleId}} — keywords: {{keywords}}. This prompt would skip the cache.
freshness.testResultNoMatch: No rule matched. This prompt would not skip the cache.
freshness.applyToggleTooltip: When ON, matching prompts skip both extract and semantic cache and go fresh to the upstream. When OFF, freshness rules still fire in logs but the cache lookup is not skipped.

statusStrip.disableTooltip: Immediately disables semantic cache fleet-wide. In-flight requests complete. New requests skip semantic cache.
statusStrip.disableConfirmDescription: This disables semantic cache cluster-wide. Existing in-flight requests finish. New requests skip semantic cache immediately. Continue?
statusStrip.confirmSemanticDescription: In-flight requests complete; new requests skip semantic cache immediately. Continue?
statusStrip.confirmExtractDescription: New requests will not be served from the exact-match cache; existing cached entries remain in Redis until TTL expiry. Continue?
statusStrip.confirmAllDescription: Both extract and semantic cache disabled simultaneously. New requests bypass gateway cache entirely. Use for incident response. Continue?

extract.cardSubtitle: Exact-match response cache. Byte-identical requests reuse the cached response instead of calling the upstream — zero false-positive risk.
extract.ttlHelp: How long a cached response stays valid. Range 60–604800 (1 minute to 7 days). Default 3600 (1 hour). Tune higher for static FAQs, lower for time-sensitive answers.
extract.ttlOutOfRange: TTL must be between 60 and 604800 seconds when enabled.
